description: Dyflo architecture-pattern index. Vendored from the four canonical catalogs (GoF via Refactoring.Guru, Fowler PoEAA, microservices.io, Hohpe EIP). This is the INDEX, not the prose — one line of intent + matchable use_when triggers + the tradeoff + a canonical URL to click through. The research stage matches a ticket's blast-radius shape against use_when. On a miss, it falls back to live retrieval (WebSearch/context7). Refresh by hand only when a source materially changes (rare).

patterns.name: Strategy
patterns.intent: Define a family of interchangeable algorithms behind one interface; pick at runtime.
use_when: multiple ways to do the same thing
growing if/else or switch on a type
patterns.tradeoff: More classes/objects; overkill if variants never grow past two.

patterns.name: Adapter
patterns.intent: Wrap an incompatible interface so existing code can use it unchanged.
use_when: integrate a third-party or legacy interface
normalize a foreign API to our shape
patterns.tradeoff: Extra indirection layer; hides the real interface, can mask leaky abstractions.

patterns.name: Facade
patterns.intent: One simplified entry point over a complex subsystem.
patterns.tradeoff: Can become a god object if it accretes responsibilities.

patterns.name: Factory Method
patterns.intent: Defer instantiation to subclasses/functions so callers don't hardcode concrete types.
use_when: decide concrete type from config or input
patterns.tradeoff: Indirection for a single product is pure ceremony (ponytail: a function often beats a hierarchy).

patterns.name: Observer
patterns.intent: Notify a set of dependents when one object changes state.
patterns.tradeoff: Update order is non-obvious; leaks if observers aren't unsubscribed. For cross-process use EIP messaging.

patterns.name: Decorator
patterns.intent: Attach responsibilities to an object dynamically by wrapping it.
patterns.tradeoff: Deep wrapping stacks are hard to debug; order matters.

patterns.name: Command
patterns.intent: Encapsulate a request as an object (params, undo, queue, log).
patterns.tradeoff: Object-per-action overhead; unnecessary for direct calls.

patterns.name: State
patterns.intent: Let an object change behavior when its internal state changes; states become classes.
patterns.tradeoff: Class explosion for simple state; a table or enum may suffice.

patterns.name: Repository
patterns.intent: Mediate between the domain and data mapping via a collection-like interface for accessing domain objects.
use_when: abstract data access from business logic
patterns.tradeoff: Another layer; thin CRUD apps get little from it.

patterns.name: Unit of Work
patterns.intent: Track objects touched in a business transaction and coordinate writing out changes and concurrency.
use_when: batch DB writes into one transaction
patterns.tradeoff: Complexity; ORMs (session/context) usually provide it already — don't reinvent.

patterns.name: Gateway
patterns.intent: An object that encapsulates access to an external system or resource behind a simple API.
use_when: wrap an external service or system
single choke point to an external dependency
patterns.tradeoff: One more indirection; trivial for a single simple call.

patterns.name: Service Layer
patterns.intent: Define an application's boundary with a layer of services that set available operations and coordinate responses.
use_when: multiple clients (UI, API, jobs) share operations
patterns.tradeoff: Can turn anemic (logic leaks out); risks a thin pass-through layer.

patterns.name: Data Transfer Object
patterns.intent: Carry data between processes to reduce the number of remote calls.
use_when: shape data for an API boundary
decouple wire format from domain model
serialize a subset of a model
patterns.tradeoff: Mapping boilerplate; in-process code rarely needs it.

patterns.name: Anti-Corruption Layer
patterns.intent: A translation layer that keeps a foreign/legacy model from leaking into your own.
use_when: keep an external model out of the domain
migrate off an old system incrementally
high blast-radius change touching a boundary to another system
patterns.tradeoff: Translation cost and duplication; needs maintenance as the foreign model changes.

patterns.name: Strangler Fig
patterns.intent: Incrementally replace a legacy system by routing slices of functionality to new code until the old is strangled.
use_when: migrate a monolith without a big-bang rewrite
route some traffic to new implementation
patterns.tradeoff: Long period running two systems; routing/facade overhead during migration.

patterns.name: API Gateway
patterns.intent: A single entry point for clients that routes, composes, and translates to backend services.
use_when: one front door for many services
patterns.tradeoff: Can become a bottleneck and a development coupling point; risks a distributed monolith.

patterns.name: Saga
patterns.intent: Manage data consistency across services with a sequence of local transactions plus compensating actions.
patterns.tradeoff: Compensation logic is hard; no isolation — must handle intermediate visible states.

patterns.name: Circuit Breaker
patterns.intent: Stop calling a failing dependency for a while to let it recover and to fail fast.
use_when: fail fast when a dependency is down
patterns.tradeoff: Tuning thresholds is fiddly; adds state and a half-open probing dance.

patterns.name: CQRS
patterns.intent: Separate the write model from one or more read models optimized for queries.
use_when: read and write loads differ sharply
patterns.tradeoff: Two models to keep consistent; big complexity tax — do not reach for it by default.

patterns.name: Event Sourcing
patterns.intent: Persist state as a sequence of events; rebuild current state by replaying them.
patterns.tradeoff: Schema evolution of events is painful; steep learning curve; usually pairs with CQRS. Rarely the lazy choice.

patterns.name: Database per Service
patterns.intent: Each service owns its data privately; others access it only via the service's API.
patterns.tradeoff: Cross-service queries and transactions get hard (see Saga/CQRS).

patterns.name: Message Router
patterns.intent: Route a message to a different channel based on its content or conditions, without changing it.
use_when: route to different handlers by type or label
patterns.tradeoff: Central routing logic can become a bottleneck and a coupling hotspot.

patterns.name: Content-Based Router
patterns.intent: Route each message to the correct recipient based on message content.
use_when: route by a field in the payload
auto vs manual lane by ticket label
patterns.tradeoff: Router must know all recipients — becomes a change magnet as routes grow.

patterns.name: Message Translator
patterns.intent: Translate one data format to another between systems that use different representations.
use_when: normalize heterogeneous inputs to one shape
map external format to internal envelope
patterns.tradeoff: Extra mapping to maintain as either side's format drifts.

patterns.name: Canonical Data Model
patterns.intent: Define a common format independent of any specific application to minimize per-integration translators.
use_when: shared internal envelope for multiple integrations
patterns.tradeoff: The canonical model itself becomes a shared dependency to govern.

patterns.name: Dead Letter Channel
patterns.intent: Move messages the system cannot process to a special channel instead of losing or blocking them.
use_when: don't block the queue on one bad item
patterns.tradeoff: Needs monitoring/replay tooling or the dead-letter channel silently fills.

patterns.name: Publish-Subscribe Channel
patterns.intent: Deliver each message to all interested subscribers via a broadcast channel.
use_when: broadcast an event to many consumers
patterns.tradeoff: Delivery guarantees and subscriber lifecycle add operational burden.

patterns.name: Idempotent Receiver
patterns.intent: Make a receiver safely handle duplicate messages so at-least-once delivery is harmless.
use_when: webhook or poll may fire twice
patterns.tradeoff: Needs a dedupe store or natural idempotency key to track processed ids.
